content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
• 428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916)322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
August 20, 1998
Ms. Wendy Stockton
City Attorney
City of Santa Maria
204 East Cook Street
Santa Maria, California 93454-5 169
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-138
Dear Ms. Stockton:
As City Attorney, you are requesting advice on behalf of the Mayor of the City of Santa
Maria. This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ Because your letter does not provide specifics regarding the
governmental decision and economic interests involved in this situation, we are treating it as a
request for informal assistance. (Regulation 18329(b), (c).)
2
QUESTION
May Mayor Maldonado participate in governmental decisions surrounding ongoing
groundwater rights litigation between the Santa Maria Valley Water Conservation District and
the City of Santa Maria?
CONCLUSION
Mayor Maldonado, who owns sizeable farming interests in the Santa Maria Valley, may
not participate in governmental decisions concerning the groundwater rights litigation, if it is
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


File No. 1-98-138
Page 2
reasonably foreseeable that decisions about the litigation will have a material financial effect on
his fanning interests, as discussed below.
FACTS
The City of Santa Maria is currently in litigation with the Santa Maria Valley Water
Conservation District and you and the previous city attorney, Art Montandon, have advised
Mayor Maldonado that he cannot participate in the decisions arising out of that litigation.
The basis for the advice you and Mr. Montandon provided is that the mayor’s family
owns and controls a closely held corporation which farms many acres of land in the Santa Maria
Valley. These holdings include the right to pump water from the groundwater basin. The
litigation between the city and the water conservation district seeks an adjudication of the
groundwater basin. This means that a final decision will determine the water rights of the city
and all other water pumpers in the valley. You state that the litigation involves millions of
dollars ofwater rights. Based on your and Mr. Montandon’s advice, the mayor has consistently
been recusing himself from decisions about the groundwater litigation.
ANALYSIS
1. Economic Interests
The Political Reform Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) As mayor of the City of Santa Maria, Mr. Maldonado is a
public official. (Section 82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest in a governmental
decision, within the meaning of Section 87100, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material fmancial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on
the official, or on a member of the official’s immediate family or on:
“(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect
investment worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest
worth one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a commercial
lending institution in the regular course ofbusiness on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.


File No. 1-98-13 8
Page 3
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts
aggregating {$290] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the
public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made....
For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, the official’s agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater.” (Section 87103.)
You have not detailed Mayor Maldonado’s economic interests except to state that his
family owns and controls a closely held corporation that farms many acres of land in the Santa
Maria Valley. Accordingly, it appears that Mayor Maldonado has an interest in this family
business that is worth $1,000 or more, and he presumably has interests in real property
(farmland) worth more than $1,000. Consequently, Mayor Maldonado may not participate in or
attempt to influence any governmental decision regarding the pending water rights litigation if it
is reasonably foreseeable that any ofhis economic interests will be affected materially by the
decisions.
2. Governmental Decisions
A public official makes a governmental decision whenever the public official votes on a
matter, commits the agency to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of the agency. (Regulation 18700(b).) Additionally, a public official participates in a
governmental decision when, acting within the authority of his or her position, the public
official:
“(1) Negotiates, without significant substantive review, with a governmental
entity or private person regarding a governmental decision ...;
(2) Advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker, either directly or
without significant intervening substantive review, by:
(A) Conducting research or making any investigation which requires the
exercise ofjudgment on the part ofthe official and the purpose of which is to
influence a governmental decision ...; or
(B) Preparing or presenting any report, analysis or opinion, orally or in writing,
which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official and the purpose
of which is to influence a governmental decision ....“ (Regulation 18700(c).)
An official is also prohibited from attempting to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a conflict of interest. With regard to a governmental decision that is within
or before an official’s agency — or an agency that is appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency — an official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of the agency for the purpose of influencing the decision. Attempts to influence a


File No. 1-98-138
Page 4
decision include appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. (Regulation 18700.1(a).) With respect to governmental decisions that are before
another agency, an official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if the official contacts members of the other agency or writes a letter on official
stationery to try to influence the other agency’s decision. (Regulation 18700.1(c).)
Accordingly, if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of Mayor Maldonado’s economic
interests will be materially affected by governmental decisions regarding the pending litigation,
he must not only disqualify himself from participating in formal decisions of the city that may
affect his economic interests, but must also refrain from influencing the decisions by
communicating with other members of city staff, the city council, or members of the Santa Maria
Valley Water Conservation District regarding the decisions.
3. Foreseeability
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. To be foreseeable, the effect of a decision must be more than a mere possibility;
however, certainty is not required. (Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Comm.
(1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983, 989-991; Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) The Act seeks to prevent more than actual conflicts of interest; it
seeks to prevent even the appearance of a possible conflict of interest. (Witt v. Morrow, supra at
823.)
You stated that the pending litigation will affect the water rights for property owners in
the area affected by the litigation. Mayor Maldonado owns a farming business that will be
affected by the outcome of the pending litigation. Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that
decisions regarding the pending litigation will have an effect on Mayor Maldonado’s economic
interests. If the effect is material, disqualification will be required.
4. Materiality
Regulation 18702 sets forth the guidelines for determining whether an official’s economic
interest in a decision is materially affected by a decision, based on whether the official’s
economic interest is directly or indirectly affected by the decision. If the official’s economic
interest is directly involved in the decision, the effect is considered material. (Regulation
18702.1.) A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision before an official’s
agency when that person or entity, either personally or by an agent initiates, is a named party in,
or the subject of the proceeding concerning the decision before the official. (Regulation
18702.1(b).) A person or business entity is the “subject of a proceeding” if a decision involves
the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the subject person or business entity. (Regulation 18702.1(b)(3).)


File No. 1-98-138
Page 5
You have not provided facts indicating that Mayor Maldonado or his business are a
named party in the litigation or the subject of the proceeding. Therefore we turn to the indirect
effect of the groundwater litigation decisions on Mayor Maldonado’ s farming business and land
ownership.
Business Interests: Mayor Maldonado’s farming business (in which he presumably has
an investment of $1,000 or more) will be affected indirectly by the pending decisions. When a
business such as Mayor Maldonado ‘S will be affected indirectly by governmental decisions,
Regulation 18702.2 (copy enclosed) sets forth the standards for determining materiality, based on
the size of the business entity.
Because you do not know the financial size of Mayor Maldonado’s farming business, you
and he will have to review regulation 18702.2 yourselves to determine the applicable threshold
for materiality. In addition, the facts you have provided do not quantify the financial effect that
decisions about the groundwater litigation are likely to have on Mayor Maldonado’s farming
business, other than to say that the litigation “involves millions of dollars in water rights.” If
decisions about the groundwater litigation will affect Mayor Maldonado ‘S farming business by
the amount specified in the appropriate subdivision ofregulation 18702.2, then he must refrain
from participating in the decisions.
Real Property: Decisions about the pending litigation may affect Mayor Maldonado’s
interests in real property indirectly. When an official’s interest in real property will be affected
indirectly by pending governmental decisions, and where there is no subject property from which
distances can be measured, the effect of the decision is considered material if the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
“(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has an interest; or
(B) Will affect the rental value of the property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.” (Regulation 1 8702.3(a)(3).)
Accordingly, if decisions regarding the pending litigation will result in changes in water
allocation that will affect Mayor Maldonado’s interests in real property in the amounts stated
above, his disqualification will be required.
Finally, it appears that decisions about the groundwater litigation will have an effect on
Mayor Maldonado’s property interests and farming business that is distinguishable from the
effect on a substantial segment of the public. Although changes in water allocation will affect all
landowners in the jurisdiction, the information you have provided does not indicate that ten
percent or more of the population in the City of Santa Maria or 50 percent of all businesses in the
city will be affected in substantially the same manner as Mayor Maldonado and his fanning
business. (Regulation 18703.) Accordingly, the “public generally” exception is not applicable in


File No. 1-98-13 8
Page 6
this situation and Mayor Maldonado’s disqualification is required if you and he determine that
the pending decisions will affect any of his economic interests materially, as discussed above.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosure
SGC:HPW:jlw
